summary: I was living in Chapel Hill when I first went to Atlanta to take the SRC job. It was a significant point in my life as I transitioned from my earlier experiences in Texas and education in Chicago. Chapel Hill was my home before moving on to new opportunities in the South.

I was born in Texas in 1901, about 45 miles northeast of Dallas, in a small farming village called Caddo Mills. Texas was where my family had settled generations before, with my grandfathers and great-grandfather pioneering the land. Growing up there shaped my early years and gave me a strong connection to southern culture and agriculture.

Dallas was the nearest large city to my hometown of Caddo Mills, about 45 miles southwest. Although I grew up in a small rural community, Dallas represented the urban center nearby and was part of the region I was familiar with in Texas. It was a reference point for where I was from.

I was born in Caddo Mills, Texas, a small farming village with about 300 people. The town was named after the Caddo Indians and was settled mostly by southern people like my family. It had rich black soil ideal for cotton and other crops, and the MK&T Railroad since the 1880s helped us market our farm products.

My great-grandfather moved to Texas during the closing year of the Lone Star Republic, around 1844. This was a pivotal time as Texas was still an independent republic before joining the United States. His migration marked the beginning of my family's deep roots in Texas.

One of my grandfathers came from Alabama and was part of a plantation family with farming experience. He brought his skills and knowledge to Texas when he settled there. Both grandfathers, including the one from Alabama, contributed to our family's agricultural background.

My other grandfather was from North Carolina and moved to Texas in 1856. He was a preacher, teacher, and farmer, and had multiple skills necessary for rural life. North Carolina was part of my family's heritage and influenced our values and education.

I was a third cousin of Lyndon B. Johnson; his grandfather and my grandfather were first cousins. Although I never met him personally, my oldest brother knew him because of his local political interests. I did see Lyndon Johnson make speeches but never had the pleasure of a personal meeting.

The MK&T Railroad had been running through Caddo Mills since the early 1880s. It was crucial for our community because it provided easy access to cities like Oklahoma, St. Louis, and Chicago. This railroad greatly enhanced our ability to market farm products and contributed to the prosperity of our small town.

Greenville was the county seat about eight miles from Caddo Mills. During cotton picking season, black labor teams were organized from Greenville and brought to our farms. It was a nearby town that played a role in the agricultural labor system of our community.

I earned a scholarship to Baylor University after attending a junior college in Greenville. I was raised Baptist, and my family had strong ties to the Baptist church, including my grandfather who helped found the Baptist church in our town. At Baylor, I initially pursued pre-ministerial studies but later shifted my focus to teaching.

I planned to attend Fort Worth Seminary after Baylor as part of my pre-ministerial training. However, I became disillusioned with the conservative atmosphere there, especially due to attacks by J. Frank Norris on more liberal Bible teachers. This experience contributed to my decision to leave the ministry path.

J. Frank Norris was a Baptist conservative minister from Fort Worth who frequently attacked the Bible Department at Baylor and my sociology teacher. His bigoted views and attacks on non-literalist teachings made my studies difficult and influenced my decision to change career paths. He was a significant figure in the religious conflicts I experienced.

My family's small libraries included works by Thomas Jefferson, which showed a broad-minded approach to education despite our conservative background. These books were part of the intellectual environment I grew up with, alongside other significant works like Darwin's Origin of Species.

My family's library contained a copy of Darwin's On the Origin of Species, which was quite remarkable given the conservative religious context. This exposure to evolutionary theory sparked my interest in broader scientific and sociological ideas. It was part of the diverse intellectual resources that influenced me growing up.

Robert E. Park was my sociology professor at the University of Chicago and a leading figure in the field. He had worked as a personal secretary to Booker T. Washington at Tuskegee and likely helped draft some of Washington's famous speeches. Park was very encouraging to me and had a special interest in race and ethnic groups.

Booker T. Washington was the leader at Tuskegee Institute where Robert E. Park worked as his personal secretary. Washington gave famous speeches, including one at the 1895 Cotton States Exposition, which Park may have helped draft. Washington's work and influence were part of the intellectual background I encountered at Chicago.

Tuskegee Institute was where Booker T. Washington led and where Robert E. Park worked as his personal secretary for several years. The institute was a center for African American education and leadership, and it indirectly influenced my sociological studies through Park's experiences. It represented a significant institution in race relations history.

The Cotton States and International Exposition of 1895 was notable for Booker T. Washington's famous speech about the 'five fingers of the hand.' I suspect Robert E. Park had a hand in drafting that speech. This event was an important historical moment in race relations and southern progress.

I attended the University of Chicago for my master's degree in sociology in 1921. It was a relatively new but prestigious institution known for its social sciences. There I studied under Robert E. Park and Edith Abbott, taking extra courses and writing my thesis on the Ku Klux Klan's resurgence.

Edith Abbott was one of my professors at the University of Chicago, teaching social statistics. I took her course along with others to fulfill my degree requirements. Her teaching was part of the rigorous academic environment that helped me complete my master's thesis.

My master's thesis at the University of Chicago focused on the resurgence of the Ku Klux Klan in the postwar period. This topic was significant in understanding race relations and social dynamics in the South after World War I. Researching the Klan gave me insight into the challenges of that era.

After graduating, I worked at the Idlewild Country Club, a wealthy Jewish country club south of Chicago. It was the only job I could find initially, and I worked there while searching for teaching positions. The club had notable families like the Fleishmans and Marx, and it provided me with room, board, and a small salary.

Ohio Wesleyan University offered me a teaching position in sociology after I struggled to find a job. Bruce Melvin, the person who called me, was impressed by my recommendations and hired me sight unseen. This opportunity marked the beginning of my academic career.

I interviewed for a position at Earlham College but was told I looked too young despite good recommendations. The president expressed confidence in my abilities but preferred someone more mature-looking at that time. Although I didn't get the job there, it was part of my early job search experience.
